content.problemStatement:
There are NNN empty seats in a row, numbered 111 to NNN.

NNN introverts arrive one by one in order from 111 to NNN and choose a seat for themselves. Being introverts, they have some rules to minimize contact with other people:


**Primary goal:** The person will aim to maximize their chosen seat's distance from the closest occupied seat.
**Secondary goal:** In case there are multiple seats satisfying the primary goal, the person will aim to choose a seat such that the next person to arrive will have a higher distance from the closest occupied seat (i.e., the person aims to help the next person meet their primary goal better).
In case multiple seats satisfy both the goals, ties are broken arbitrarily.
Formally, if the set of occupied seat numbers is SSS, and the set of all seat numbers is U={1,2,…,N}U = \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}U={1,2,…,N}, then:


**Primary goal:** Choose x∈U∖Sx \in U \setminus Sx∈U∖S to maximize min⁡ i ∈ S {∣x−i∣}\min_{\text{ } i \text{ } \in \text{ } S \text{ }}\{|x - i|\}min i ∈ S ​{∣x−i∣}.
**Secondary goal:** Let CCC denote the set of all xxx maximizing min⁡ i ∈ S {∣x−i∣}\min_{\text{ } i \text{ } \in \text{ } S \text{ }}\{|x - i|\}min i ∈ S ​{∣x−i∣}.

Choose any y∈Cy \in Cy∈C such that min⁡ i ∈ S ∪ {y} {∣x−i∣}\min_{\text{ } i \text{ } \in \text{ } S \text{ } \cup \text{ } \{y\} \text{ } }\{|x - i|\}min i ∈ S ∪ {y} ​{∣x−i∣} over all x∈U∖(S∪{y})x \in U \setminus (S \cup \{y\})x∈U∖(S∪{y}) is maximized.
If we denote PiP_iPi​ to be the number of the person sitting in seat number iii, then P=[P1,P2,…,PN]P = [P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N]P=[P1​,P2​,…,PN​] forms a permutation of the integers 111 to NNN.

Given a permutation PPP of the integers 111 to NNN, determine if it is possible for NNN introverts to seat themselves according to the above rules such that their final arrangement forms the permutation PPP.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of introverts.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ — the permutation.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `"YES"` (without quotes) if it is possible for the introverts to sit in the given arrangement while following their rules, and `"NO"` (without quotes) otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e., the strings `NO`, `nO`, `No`, and `no` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: P=[2,1]P = [2, 1]P=[2,1] can be generated by these steps:

For the 1st1^{\text{st}}1st person, both the seats fulfill both the goals.
So he can arbitrarily choose seat 111 or seat 222. In this situation, he chooses seat 222.
The 2nd2^{\text{nd}}2nd person has only one choice of seat, so he chooses seat 111.

Test case 222: P=[1,4,3,6,5,2]P = [1, 4, 3, 6, 5, 2]P=[1,4,3,6,5,2] can be generated by these steps:

The 1st1^{\text{st}}1st person can sit in any of the five seats to fulfill the primary goal, but to fulfill the secondary goal he can only choose seat 111 or seat 666 (so that the 2nd2^{\text{nd}}2nd person can get a higher maximum closest distance of 555 by sitting in the opposite seat (666 or 111 respectively). In this situation, the 1st1^{\text{st}}1st person chooses seat 111.
The 2nd2^{\text{nd}}2nd person will choose to sit in seat 666, as it has the highest closest distance to another person (this distance is 555).
The 3rd3^{\text{rd}}3rd person can choose to sit in seat 333 or seat 444 to fulfill the primary goal, as both have the highest closest distance to another person (this distance is 222). Both choices will give an equal maximum closest distance of 111 for the 4th4^{\text{th}}4th person, so the choice is arbitrary. In this situation, the 3rd3^{\text{rd}}3rd person chooses seat 333.
The 4th4^{\text{th}}4th person can choose to sit in seat 222, seat 444 or seat 555 to fulfill the primary goal, as all have the highest closest distance to another person (this distance is 111). All choices will give an equal maximum closest distance of 111 for the 5th5^{\text{th}}5th person, so the choice is arbitrary. In this situation, the 4th4^{\text{th}}4th person chooses seat 222.
The 5th5^{\text{th}}5th person can choose to sit in seat 444 or seat 555 to fulfill the primary goal, as both have the highest closest distance to another person (this distance is 111). All choices will give an equal maximum closest distance of 111 for the 6th6^{\text{th}}6th person, so the choice is arbitrary. In this situation, the 5th5^{\text{th}}5th person chooses seat 555.
The 6th6^{\text{th}}6th person has only one choice of seat, so he chooses seat 444.

Test case 333: P=[1,7,3,5,4,6,2]P = [1, 7, 3, 5, 4, 6, 2]P=[1,7,3,5,4,6,2] cannot be generated by the rules:

The 1st1^{\text{st}}1st person can sit in any of the seven seats to fulfill the primary goal, but to fulfill the secondary goal he can only choose seat 111 or seat 777 (so that the 2nd2^{\text{nd}}2nd person can get a higher maximum closest distance of 666 by sitting in the opposite seat (555 or 111 respectively). In this situation, the 1st1^{\text{st}}1st person chooses seat 111.
The 2nd2^{\text{nd}}2nd person will choose to sit in seat 777, as it has the highest closest distance to another person (this distance is 666).
The 3rd3^{\text{rd}}3rd person can only choose to sit in seat 444, as it has the highest closest distance (this distance is 333). But according to the given permutation ppp, the 3rd3^{\text{rd}}3rd person chose seat 222, which is not possible.
